title: Planning before documenting
description: How to plan a community language documentation project.
content: ## Module 1 ### Planning before documenting --- ## Trainer Goal Help participants plan from their own lives and communities, not from outside. By the end, each participant should have a **simple, written plan** covering: - What is missing and needs documentation - Who they will work with - What they will document and why it matters - Where the recording might live long-term --- ## 1.1 Which topics are missing *Trainer: Ganesh Birua* Ask participants to think about their own village, town, or community and list: - Topics not yet recorded — a ritual, festival, farming or forest work, a local issue, a craft, women's work, migration - Elders and others who hold important knowledge, songs, or stories - Who holds power between the documenter and the interviewee? - Places that carry memory — a river, shrine, meeting place, market, forest path > "What do we know is important, but we do not yet have in audio or video?" ___ ### 1.1 Exercise Ask participants to write down **3 things**: 1. A topic or area that is not yet documented but should be 2. One or two people you already know who can speak about it 3. A place where you might record them --- ## 1.2 Who to interview and how *Trainer: Ganesh Birua* How documenters identify people to interview: - **Asking around** — who is known for songs, stories, rituals, farming knowledge, or memories? - **Listening** in meetings, festivals, or everyday conversations to see who explains things clearly and with care ___ ### 1.2 Approaching people respectfully - **First contact** — visit, phone call, or message to introduce yourself and explain why you are interested - **Meeting** — sit with them, explain what you are doing, who you are doing it with, and what will happen to the recording It is fine if someone says **no** or wants to think. Planning includes giving people **time and choice**. --- ## 1.3 Planned vs On-the-go recording *Trainer: Ramjit Tudu* Documentation happens in two ways — both are valid: | Type | When | |------|------| | **Planned visits** | Documenter fixes a time and place in advance | | **On-the-go** | Documenter sees or hears something important while already somewhere — a field, festival, bus, protest | ___ ### 1.3 Tips for on-the-go recording - Keep your phone or recorder **charged** with free space - Make a **quick note** of date, place, and names immediately after - Think of on-the-go recordings as **part of the plan**, not as mistakes > "If you see or hear something important and safe to record, it is better to record quickly and follow up later with a calmer, planned conversation." --- ## 1.4 Where will recordings live? *Trainer: Ramjit Tudu* Three types of "homes" for recordings: 1. **Community spaces** — local groups, libraries, youth clubs, schools, community centres 2. **Partner platforms** — organisations such as PARI and archives that work closely with communities 3. **Large platforms and archives** — Wikimedia projects, regional or national archives, specialist archives ___ ### 1.4 What to prioritise - **Community spaces come first** — the first responsibility is to the people whose voices and images are recorded - External archives and large platforms can be added **later**, after consent is clear and people are comfortable - **Discuss sensitive topics** with the community — record but give access only to the community Ask participants: - "If I record this, where in my community can people listen or watch it later?" - "Which partner organisation or platform could be a good second home, if any?" --- ## 1.5 Consent, Safety, and Labour *Trainer: Shalini A* Three questions to connect planning to action: 1. **Consent** — "How will I explain clearly what I am doing and where the recording may go?" 2. **Safety** — "Could this recording put anyone at risk if it is shared? How can I reduce that risk?" 3. **Labour** — "Who is giving their time and knowledge? How will we recognise and compensate this work?" ___ ### 1.5 What is consent? An agreement or permission to engage in an activity — a safe, open, comfortable conversation between all parties. Consent must be: - **Affirmative** — clear words or actions - **Voluntary** — given freely, without pressure - **Informed** — person understands what will happen - **Specific** — for this purpose, not everything - **Withdrawable** — at any point, by informing each party ___ ### 1.5 Types of consent | Type | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Implied** | Given by a person's action or gesture | | **Express** | Directly and clearly given with explicit words | | **Informed** | Fully informed process, often with a signed document | | **Unanimous** | Complete agreement among multiple parties | ___ ### 1.5 Recording consent **What it means:** - Permission to officially capture and store audio, video, and images - Communities or individuals must be **fully aware** of how their knowledge will be recorded, stored, and used - **Ownership** — ensure recordings remain with the knowledge holder **Audience question:** *What if the information is already used by others and they want to withdraw it?* ___ ### 1.5 Safety risks Sharing private, confidential, or non-consensual recordings can cause: - Legal consequences, reputational damage, and harm to emotional wellbeing - Harassment, bullying, blackmail, or public shame - Employment troubles - Physical safety risks — exposing personal identity, location, enabling stalking - Misinformation — media manipulated or clipped out of context - Data breaches — sensitive information exposed permanently ___ ### 1.5 Reducing safety risks - **Evaluate first** — is sharing absolutely necessary? - **Know your audience** — who can access the data and what will they use it for? - **Use secure channels** — implement strong security practices - **Anonymize** — mask names, locations, voices, blur faces where needed - **Restrict access** — share on secure, private platforms rather than publicly - **Delete after use** — remove recordings and copies when no longer needed - **Watermarks** — can prohibit redistribution - *Legally, you can invoke Right to Privacy under Article 21* ___ ### 1.5 Labour — who contributes? **Who gives time and knowledge:** - Individuals or groups from the community being documented - Individuals or groups who do the documenting **Who organises and travels:** - **Elders and knowledge keepers** — preserve and pass down community knowledge - **Researchers and institutions** — local and global, formal documentation - **Regional NGOs** — train community members, organise and publish local knowledge (e.g. Keystone Foundation) ___ ### 1.5 Recognition and compensation - **Respect ownership** — the community owns their data - **Secure consent** before documenting, recording, or publishing their knowledge - **Always credit** specific individuals and communities — do not generalise to anonymous groups - **Fair compensation** — direct financial remuneration, community capacity building, employing community members as paid researchers, project leaders, and co-authors — not just subjects --- ## Trainer Activity For their first few planned recordings, ask participants to note: 1. Who will be the speaker(s)? 2. Who will accompany or help during the recording? 3. Any worries they already see — sensitive topics, borders, land conflict, gender-based risk These notes will be used again in **Module 2** when they learn specific ways to record consent, decide access levels, and think about payment. ___ ### Before next session Individuals or groups can **edit this plan online** after the session and share it in the group chat, then ask others for feedback. **Labour division for this module:** - Co-trainers: Ganesh Birua, Ramjit Tudu, Shalini A (sections 1.3, 1.4) See it fullscreen

title: Prepare people and space
description: How to prepare your speaker, location, and recording conditions before pressing record.

title: Consent, ethics and payment
description: How to handle consent, credit, and payment fairly.
content: Check your understanding --- primary_color: "#3d6b8e" secondary_color: "#e8f0f7" text_color: "#1a1a1a" shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Before recording someone, when should you ask for their consent? > Consent must happen before the recording begins — not during or after. - [ ] After the recording is done and you are happy with it - [ ] Only if you plan to share the recording publicly - [x] Before the recording begins, every time - [ ] Only for video, not for audio ## Which of these is an example of informed consent? > Informed consent means the person clearly understands what will be recorded, where it will go, and who will hear or see it. - [ ] The person nods when you point your phone at them - [ ] You tell them it is "just for practice" even though you plan to use it - [x] You explain what you are recording, where it may be shared, and they agree in their own words - [ ] You get permission from the community leader on behalf of the speaker ## Can a person withdraw their consent after a recording is made? - [ ] No, once recorded the material belongs to the project - [ ] Only within 24 hours of the recording - [x] Yes, at any point, and you must respect that wish - [ ] Only if they signed a written form ## Which of these describes fair payment for a knowledge holder's time? > Recognition and compensation should reflect the value of the knowledge and labour contributed. - [ ] A thank-you message on social media - [ ] No payment is needed — sharing knowledge is a community duty - [ ] Paying only the community leader, who distributes as they see fit - [x] Direct payment to the individual, or a clearly agreed form of recognition they have chosen ## Who owns a recording of someone sharing their traditional knowledge? - [ ] The organisation funding the documentation project - [ ] The person who operated the recording device - [x] The knowledge holder and their community - [ ] Whoever uploads it first to an online platform

title: Contributing to the Data Literacy Training Course
description: A guide to setting up and contributing to the project from scratch.
content:
This guide walks through setting up the project from scratch on a new machine.
Editing content via “Edit this page” Every page on this site shows an Edit this page link at the bottom. This is the quickest way to fix a typo, update outdated content, or add a paragraph — no local setup required.
How it works Open the page you want to edit and click Edit this page at the bottom. GitHub opens the file in its web editor. If you don’t have write access to the repository, GitHub automatically forks the repo into your account. Make your changes in the editor. Scroll to Commit changes, write a short description of what you changed, and select Create a new branch and open a pull request. Click Propose changes. GitHub shows a diff and opens the pull request form. Add a title and description, then click Create pull request. A maintainer reviews the pull request, asks questions if needed, and merges it. The site rebuilds automatically on merge via GitHub Actions.
What the link points to The link is constructed from the editURL in config.toml:
editURL = "https://github.com/ofdn/clda/edit/main/content/" For a page at content/Module_1/intro.md the link becomes:
https://github.com/ofdn/clda/edit/main/content/Module_1/intro.md Tips Keep edits focused — one topic per pull request makes review faster. The page content is plain Markdown. Headings use ##, bold uses **text**, and links use [label](url). Front matter at the top of each file (between +++ markers) controls the page title and sidebar order — leave it unchanged unless you intend to rename the page. If you want to preview your changes locally before opening a pull request, follow the local setup steps below. Localisation Hugo handles multilingual sites through two mechanisms: content translation (parallel Markdown files) and UI string translation (i18n files).
Translate content files For each page you want to translate, create a copy of the Markdown file with the language code inserted before .md:
content/Module_1/_index.md ← English (default) content/Module_1/_index.fr.md ← French content/Module_1/_index.hi.md ← Hindi The front matter title and body text should be fully translated. Hugo automatically links the language variants together and shows a language switcher in the nav.
Translate UI strings Create i18n/fr.toml and i18n/hi.toml alongside the existing i18n/en.toml and translate each string:
# i18n/hi.toml [Edit-this-page] other = "इस पृष्ठ को संपादित करें" [Search-placeholder] other = "खोजें..." Test the localised build hugo server # French version: http://localhost:1313/fr/ # Hindi version: http://localhost:1313/hi/ Pages not yet translated fall back to the English version automatically.

title: Recording audio and video
description: How to use phones and other devices to capture clear sound and picture.

title: Emotional safety while recording
description: How to protect the wellbeing of speakers and documenters during and after a recording session.

title: How to record clear audio and video
description: How to record clear audio and video in real-world conditions.
content:
Trainer goal Help participants understand how to record clear, honest audio and video with the tools they already have, in ways that respect people’s comfort and safety. 3.1 Prepare people and space (Trainer:akumbu@endangeredlanguages.com) Your consultants (speakers)
Approach your speakers and introduce the recording session in a respectful and inviting manner. Specify all aspects of the recording process (format) and time required. Pay attention to whether your consultants are loud or soft speakers. Recording location Select the best location for optimal sound quality. Minimize background noise, e.g., low-key hums from household appliances like refrigerators. Avoid being too close to a road, bird nests, children’s playing ground, etc. Inform people around you, so they don’t interrupt your session when you are recording. For video make sure to station your camera in a way as to capture the speakers fully, or get the best angle on them. Having a proper view will allow you to capture gestures, which usually contain important cultural information. Recording conditions Make the speakers feel comfortable (sitting, food, water, childcare, etc) Inform them that you can pause or stop recording to allow them time for themselves if needed. 3.2 Use phones and other devices well (trainer: akumbu@endangeredlanguages.com) Recording equipment settings
Quality recordings can be made with phones, audio recorders, or video cameras. Make good use of what you have. Usually, your phone settings will be good for audio/video recordings. Check the settings on your audio/video recording devices to ensure you are capturing sound properly and satisfactorily. If using external microphones (recommended), check that all connections and settings allow for sound to be captured. Stationing recording equipment Setting up your recording device relative to the speaker(s) is crucial. If using a phone or audio recorder, set it up on a table between you and the speaker(s). If using an external microphone with recording device, make sure you have both the recording device and microphone in good locations or range to pick up the speaker’s voice. If you have headphones, use them check if the sound if adequate. If you must hold your phone in your hand do your best to keep it steady. Nice if you have tripod stand for video camera. Move your device gently/slowly if needed to capture certain actions. Check lighting because it can make or break the quality of your video. Experiment with different lighting setups and find what works best for you. Checking battery, storage, and backups before recording.
Begin recording on full battery charge. Ensure your storage SD card has sufficient space to carry your recording. A 128GB SD card recording on 1080p resolution at 60 fps can hold approximately 10 hours of footage. It is best to back up previous recordings on external hard drives rather than accumulating all in a single SD card. 3.3 Audio/Sound: clear voice (trainer: akumbu@endangeredlanguages.com) Distance between mouth and microphone; avoiding rubbing noise. For clear sound, be careful about the distance between the microphone and the speaker’s mouth. For example, if using a lavalier microphone, place it approximately 15-30cm (6-12 inches) away from your speaker’s mouth. Managing background sounds (traffic, animals, loudspeakers) without erasing local life. As said earlier, background noise may interfere and affect audio quality. To avoid this, check carefully and take necessary measures. Doing a short test recording and listening back together. Before you begin recording, do a few tests to determine the best spot to place your microphone and recording device. Be sure that you can hear your speaker(s) clearly, and see all possible meaningful gestures.
3.4 Picture: frame, light, and movement Framing a person with dignity (not from above, not cutting the head, no clutter that distracts). Using natural light: facing the light, avoiding strong backlight or directly under sun. Set the video frame in a way that the person being recorded does not move out of it during natural movement. When to move the camera and when to keep it still; showing hands, other things, and surroundings when needed. Discuss hand cues like thumbs up, short clap to communicate non-verbally in advance. 3.5 Emotional safety while recording Not pushing people to share painful memories; giving them control. Sharing own stories to create a sense of equality. Stopping or pausing when someone is tired or upset. Recognising that the trainer may also carry emotional load and needs support. 3.6 Trainer activity: “record, review, reflect” Make pairs, each pair records a 3–5 minute conversation in a local language. Watch or listen together and note: one thing that worked well, one thing to improve for sound, one for picture, one for comfort. Ask: “Will you be happy if this was shared widely?” and discuss why or why not. Reading resource PARI Guidelines

title: How to manage files safely and processing audio and video
description: How to manage and back up files safely, and process audio and video.
content: This chapter is on everyday reality (phones, memory cards, slow or breaking internet) with simple archiving habits to help long-term preservation. Trainer goal Help participants keep their recordings safe, each file named clearly, and easy to find later, even with limited devices and connectivity. 4.1 Understand what you already have Simple self-audit: list audio, video, photos already recorded in the community. Group discussion: where are these stored now (phone, laptop, WhatsApp, social media)? Risks: lost phones, deleted chat history, broken hard drives.

title: How to edit audio and video for sharing
description: How to edit media for sharing and long-term use.

title: How to prepare media and metadata for Wikimedia Commons
description: How to prepare media and metadata for Wikimedia Commons, Wikidata, Wikipedia, and other Wikimedia projects.

title: How to share materials with archives, libraries, and communities
description: How to share materials with archives, libraries, and communities.
content: Key points: How to contact archives and libraries and deposit media for archiving. How to share copies with the community. How to plan follow-up support after the workshop. Trainer goal Help participants understand where their materials can live in the long term and how to share them safely and respectfully. By the end, each participant should know at least one archive they could approach, and have a simple plan for sharing copies with their community after the workshop. 7.1 Types of places that can hold your materials Give examples of where language materials can be kept: Preservation on websites, social media, privately (most cases). Community archives (run by a local organisation or group). Public libraries, university libraries, and cultural organisations. Digital language archives (examples: AILLA, ELAR, Kaipuleohone). Wikimedia projects (Commons, Wikidata, Wikipedia) as public access layers. Explain that different places have different rules: Some archives take only certain formats or languages. Some require deposit agreements or specific licenses. Some community archives prefer “post-custodial” models: communities own materials legally, institution supports storage and access. 7.2 How to contact an archive or library Encourage participants to contact archives early. Suggest basic steps for first contact: Find an email or contact form for the archive or library. Introduce who you are and what community you work with. Explain the type of materials you have (audio, video, photos, text) and in which language(s). Ask if they are open to community deposits or partnerships. Suggest what information to include: Project or collection title. Languages and place. Types and formats of files (for example, .wav, .mp4, .jpg). Rough amount of data (number of files or approximate size). Any access limits or consent conditions. 7.3 Preparing for deposit Explain how institutional archives and libraries will ask for: A basic description of the collection (who, where, when, what). A list of files with titles and dates. Information about language(s) and people involved. Clear statements about rights, consent, and access levels. Show how participants’ existing work can help: Their folder structure and file naming make it easier to share. Their metadata logs can be adapted into a deposit form or catalogue entry. Their consent notes and access ladder help archives understand restrictions. Discuss with archive/library for “progressive deposit”: They can send small batches over time, instead of waiting for a “perfect” collection. This approach appears in several language archiving recommendations. 7.4 Sharing copies with the community Stress that community sharing is as important as formal deposit: Community members should be able to listen, watch, and use the materials if they wish. Suggest practical ways to share copies: Local listening or viewing sessions. Copies on USB drives or memory cards for community elders or schools. Closed-access WhatsApp or Telegram groups for sharing selected clips. Printed transcriptions or booklets for schools or community centres. Encourage a feedback loop: Ask community members what is useful and what feels sensitive. Adjust future documentation and sharing based on this feedback.

title: Glossary
description: Key terms and definitions for community language documentation and archiving.
content:
This glossary lists preferred terms and terms to avoid when writing about languages, communities, and identities in the context of community language documentation and archiving.
Language terms Terms to use Terms to avoid Adivasi language Tribal language Local language Vernacular Mother tongue Minority language Low-resource language Dialect / बोली Note on “dialect” and बोली: These terms carry a hierarchy that implies the language is lesser or incomplete. Prefer “language”, “mother tongue”, or “local language” unless the speaker themselves uses these words to describe their own speech.
Community and identity terms Terms to use Terms to avoid Dalit Caste slurs such as “chamar”, “chahar”, “Harijan” — do not use at all unless directly quoting someone; ensure community dignity is maintained Savarna Upper-caste Women Female Men Male Note on caste slurs: Words such as “chamar”, “chahar”, and “Harijan” are deeply harmful. Do not use them in documentation, captions, metadata, or descriptions. If you must quote someone who used such a word, make clear it is a direct quotation and add a note about the term’s impact.
Note on “female” / “male”: These are biological/medical classifications. When referring to people in social or community contexts, use “women” and “men”.

title: Credits
description: contributors and packages used by hugo-theme-docdock
content:
Contributors Module co-authors:
Ganesh Birua is a Ho language activist from Mayurbhanj, Odisha. Since 2017, he has created hundreds of educational images, run the Warang Chiti Project 2030, and collaborated with OpenSpeaks Archives on transcriptions and translations. गणेश विरुआ मयूरभंज, ओडिशा के एक हो भाषा कार्यकर्ता हैं। 2017 से उन्होंने सैकड़ों शैक्षिक चित्र बनाए हैं, और वारंग चिटी प्रोजेक्ट 2030 चलाते हैं, और ट्रांसक्रिप्शन और अनुवाद के लिए OpenSpeaks Archives के साथ सहयोग किया है। Ramjit Tudu is a Santali language activist and technologist from Mayurbhanj, Odisha. He co-founded Ol Chiki Tech, created the Birmali e-magazine, and is a key contributor to Santali Wikipedia. रामजीत टुडु मयूरभंज, ओडिशा के एक संथाली भाषा के कार्यकर्ता और प्रौद्योगिकी विशेषज्ञ हैं। उन्होंने ओल चिकि टेक की सह-स्थापना की, बिर्माली ई-मैगजीन बनाई, और संथाली विकिपीडिया में एक प्रमुख योगदानकर्ता हैं। 3. Shalini A is a community archivist and technologist at Janastu and Servelots. She also leads community Wi-Fi mesh and low-literacy projects, and created the BaaApp and Follow the Sheep initiatives with shepherd communities. शलिनी ए जनस्तु और सर्वलोटस में एक सामुदायिक अभिलेखागार और प्रौद्योगिकी विशेषज्ञ हैं। वह सामुदायिक वाई-फाई मेष और कम साक्षरता परियोजनाओं का भी नेतृत्व करती हैं, और उन्होंने चरवाहा समुदायों के साथ BaaApp और Follow the Sheep पहलों का निर्माण किया।

title: Data Literacy Training Course
description: A workshop series on recording and archiving Adivasi, Tribal and mother tongues and local languages with lesser resources.
content:
This is a workshop series on recording and archiving Adivasi, Tribal and mother tongues and local languages with lesser resources (there is more resource for official languages like Hindi, Tamil, Bangla, Kannada, Gujarati and Telugu). Apply before July 15 if you already record and archive such languages, or want to start. Less experience is okay.
The series has:
seven online workshops (May–August 2026) one in‑person workshop in Kochi, Kerala (3 September 2026) Apply here
Steps Check eligibility. Apply from link above. There will be 2 types of sessions: 7 online sessions from May to August, and an in-person workshop in Kochi, Kerala on 3 September 2026. Select the one you can attend. Each online session will last 1 hour. It will happen on Mondays (18 May, 1 June, 15 June, 29 June, 13 July, 27 July and 10 August) at 6 in evening. Eligibility You record or plan to record Adivasi/Tribal/other mother tongues with lesser resources (preference will be given to native speakers). You are a Wikimedian interested in audio‑video documentation of such languages. If You Get Selected If attending online, you will attend at least 1 full session. If attending in Kochi, you will attend at least 3 full online sessions and in person from 9 AM to 6 PM. Online/in-person sessions will be interactive, not lectures. You are required to read and participate actively. You will contribute to Wikimedia projects and share some work in a safe and ethical way. What You Will Learn In audio‑video recording and archiving:
18 May: How to plan a community language documentation project. 1 Jun: How to handle consent, credit, and payment fairly. 15 Jun: How to record clear audio and video in real‑world conditions. 29 June: How to manage and back up files safely. 13 July: How to edit media for sharing and long‑term use. 27 July: How to prepare media and metadata for Wikimedia Commons, Wikidata, Wikipedia, and other Wikimedia projects. 10 August: How to share materials with archives, libraries, and communities. Schedule Online Workshops There are seven online workshops. Each workshop is 1 hour, on a Monday, at 6 PM India time.
Dates
18 May 1 June 15 June 29 June 13 July 27 July 10 August Each online session includes:
short talk or demonstration hands‑on exercise (using your own project or sample files) discussion and feedback to improve tools and guides In‑Person Workshop (Kochi) Date and time: 7 September, 9 AM – 5 PM India time
Location: Kochi, Kerala, India
In this workshop you will train in detail in a practical environment. You will practice with others on recording, metadata, subtitling, and Wikimedia uploads. You will test OpenSpeaks tools, framework, and training materials. Support For all selected participants:
training materials practical examples and templates free and open‑source software and user guides practice files and exercises a full curriculum if you want to train others For selected in‑person participants in Kochi:
round‑trip travel to Kochi (see application for travel rules) one night of shared hotel stay food during the workshop day Details on any trainer or participant remuneration will be added to this page once confirmed.
Outputs The series will produce open resources, and we will share them with you:
a curriculum for community language documentation and archiving, in simple English and ready for translation recorded talks and tool demonstrations (online and in‑person sessions) documentation for OpenSpeaks tools, including a subtitling tool updated guidance for a framework to help plan your documentation and archiving a tested text style guide for captioning, subtitling and transcribing example workflows from recording to Wikimedia upload and external archives You will be credited as authors for the materials you create or improve.
Outcome When we finish this series, participants will very well know how to document and archive important things from their community in audio and video. They will also know how to contribute some outputs to Wikimedia projects. They will have practical knowledge of tools and educational guides for these. This workshop model will be clearly written here. Anyone can organise a similar workshop for their own community using this model. Work in Progress We are working on these:
fairly compensate for labour and mobile/internet data to all participants create video tutorials for trainers, as reading and understanding in text is hard for many a detailed plan on how we want to engage with all interested participants after workshop and the support available to them Resources We will use and test resources from the Community Language Documentation and Archiving Training, OpenSpeaks on Wikiversity, the Oral History Framework, OpenSpeaks tools (especially Subtitler), and the OpenSpeaks Text Style Guide.
Volunteers Sign up by adding your name below to volunteer. Volunteering roles include: a) coordinating online/offline sessions, b) translating materials, and c) interpreting live sessions.
Ganesh Adimalupu (Telugu) — Interested in coordinating online/offline sessions. Bodi Baski (Santali) — Interested in translating and interpreting content into Santali. Collaborators Organised by: OpenSpeaks
Collaborators: FOSS United, Whose Knowledge?, Adivasi Lives Matter, Endangered Languages Project
Supported by: Wikimedia Foundation
